summaries.content: This lesson introduces place value for numbers up to 5 digits. Students learn that each digit in a number has a value depending on its position. From right to left, the places are: Ones, Tens, Hundreds, Thousands, and Ten-Thousands. For example, in 24,653, the 2 is in the ten-thousands place (worth 20,000), the 4 is in the thousands place (worth 4,000), and so on. Students understand that 10 ones make a ten, 10 tens make a hundred, 10 hundreds make a thousand, and 10 thousands make a ten-thousand.
keyPoints: Place values from right to left: Ones, Tens, Hundreds, Thousands, Ten-Thousands
The value of a digit depends on its position in the number
10 of one place value make 1 of the next higher place value
The smallest 4-digit number is 1,000 and the largest is 9,999

summaries.content: This lesson teaches students how to read and write 4-digit and 5-digit numbers. Students learn to write numbers in three ways: standard form (using digits like 7,305), expanded form (7,000 + 300 + 0 + 5), and number names (seven thousand three hundred and five). The lesson also covers the Indian system of placing commas. Students practise converting between these different forms.
keyPoints: Expanded form shows each digit's value: 7,000 + 300 + 5
Number name writes the number in words
In the Indian system, the first comma comes after the hundreds place

summaries.content: This lesson covers comparing and ordering large numbers. To compare two numbers, first check how many digits each has. A number with more digits is always greater. If both have the same number of digits, compare them from the leftmost digit. Students also learn to use the symbols > (greater than), < (less than), and = (equal to). They practise arranging numbers in ascending (smallest to largest) and descending (largest to smallest) order.
keyPoints: A number with more digits is always greater
If digit count is the same, compare from the leftmost digit
> means greater than, < means less than, = means equal to
Ascending order goes up; descending order goes down

summaries.content: This lesson focuses on estimation and rounding with large numbers. Students learn that estimation means making a smart guess that is close to the exact answer. They learn the rounding rule: if the digit to the right of the rounding place is 5 or more, round up; if it is less than 5, round down. Students also explore large numbers in real life, such as the population of a town, distances between cities, and prices of things like bikes and TVs.
keyPoints: Estimation gives a number that is close but not exact
Rounding rule: 5 or more = round up, less than 5 = round down
We can round to the nearest ten, hundred, or thousand
Large numbers are used for populations, distances, prices, and more
